note_content:
# Q3 Product Roadmap

## Phase 1 (July)
- Launch collaborative editing with real-time cursors
- Migrate auth from Firebase to Clerk
- Ship markdown preview with syntax highlighting

## Phase 2 (August)
- AI-powered search using embeddings
- Public sharing with request-access flow
- Dashboard with weekly activity tracking

## Phase 3 (September)
- Mobile responsive redesign
- Offline support with service workers
- Performance audit and bundle optimization

## Risks
- Clerk migration might break existing sessions
- Embedding model costs unclear at scale
- Mobile redesign scope could creep

## Notes
Check with backend team on WebSocket capacity before committing to real-time cursors.

ai_output.title: Q3 Product Roadmap Overview
ai_output.summary: Three-phase Q3 roadmap spanning July to September. Phase 1 covers collaborative editing and auth migration, Phase 2 adds AI search and sharing, Phase 3 focuses on mobile and performance. Key risks include auth migration breakage and embedding costs.
action_items: Launch collaborative editing with real-time cursors in July
Migrate authentication from Firebase to Clerk
Implement AI-powered search using embeddings in August
Build public sharing with request-access flow
Complete mobile responsive redesign in September
Check with backend team on WebSocket capacity

note_content:
# Architecture Decision Record: State Management

## Context
Our React application currently uses a mix of useState, useContext, and prop drilling for state management. As the app grows to 40+ components, this approach creates maintenance issues:
- Props are drilled 4-5 levels deep in the notes workspace
- Multiple useContext providers wrap the app tree, causing unnecessary re-renders
- No clear pattern for async server state vs. local UI state

## Decision
Adopt a two-layer approach:
1. **Server state**: Use Convex's reactive queries directly. They auto-cache, auto-invalidate, and provide real-time updates. No need for React Query or SWR since Convex handles this natively.
2. **Local UI state**: Keep React useState/useReducer for ephemeral UI concerns (modal open/close, form inputs, animation state). No global store needed.

## Consequences
- Positive: Dramatically simpler data flow. Components subscribe to exactly the data they need via `useQuery(api.notes.list)`. No manual cache invalidation.
- Positive: Real-time collaboration comes for free — Convex pushes updates to all subscribers.
- Negative: Tighter coupling to Convex. Migrating away would require replacing every useQuery call.
- Negative: Offline support needs Convex's upcoming offline mode (not yet GA).

## Status
Accepted. Migrating existing useContext-based data fetching to Convex queries.

## References
- Convex docs: https://docs.convex.dev/
- React state management comparison: https://react.dev/learn/managing-state

ai_output.title: ADR State Management Architecture
ai_output.summary: Architecture decision to adopt a two-layer state management approach: Convex reactive queries for server state (auto-caching, real-time updates) and React useState for local UI state. This eliminates prop drilling and unnecessary re-renders but creates tighter Convex coupling.
action_items: Migrate existing useContext-based data fetching to Convex queries
Remove unnecessary React Context providers from app tree
Document Convex query patterns for team onboarding
Monitor Convex offline mode GA for future offline support
